title: 第 48 節
en: This brilliant being produced by friction by Matarisvan, the messenger of Vivasvat, is clearly Agni. But in this case, as in that of Atharvan, we do not find Bhrigu any longer as the name of a god, but only as that of his worshippers, the old race of the Bhrigus. These Bhrigus are said to have discovered Agni (X, 46, 2), to have brought him to men (I, 58, 6), to have kept him in the wood (VI, 15, 2). They received him from Matarisvan (the wind), who lighted him when he was hidden (III, 5, 10). In later times the BhWgus were chiefly known as a famous family in India, like the Aiigiras and the Atharvans (Bv. X, 14, 6), and many Rishis, like #aunaka, and heroes, such as Parasurama, were proud of belonging to it. When Krishna wants to say that he is or was the best of everything, he says that among the Rishis he was BhWgu (Bhagavadgita X, 25). In all this there may be hidden traces of Vl] BHi?/GAVAATA. 809 totemism, though I must confess I cannot see them. The etymology of the name was known to the ancient Vedic interpreters, and Yaska (Nir. Ill, 17) says : ArAishi 'Bh.riguh sambabhuva, Bh/vgur bhrigyamano na dehe^ngareshu, Aiigira angara, ankana, ' BhWgu was born in the light, Bhn'gu though being fried or scorched was not burnt in the coals. The Angiras are the Aiigaras (coals), they are so-called because they mark.'

Differing from Kuhn (p. 18) and others, I take dehe not as a locative of deha, but as the perf. pass, of dah, he was burnt. The root hhrig or bhr&gg (bhriggsiti) which means to roast, to fry, in Sanskrit, and from which Yaska derives BhWgu, is, if not exactly the same, at least closely connected with other roots, meaning to shine or to blaze, such as Sk. bhr%, to shine, also bhargas, splendour, Greek (f>\eyo) (<f>\6£), Lat. fulgere (fulgur), flagrare (flamma). To heat and to shine are cognate concepts, as in suryas tapati, the sun shines, lit. the sun warms, and the act of frying was taken as an act of heating and broiling, not yet of cooking or boiling.

If Agni could be called blmgavawa, brilliant, or BhWgu, we can easily understand now how that name became the name of the ancestor of a race, being called the BhWgus, because they possessed the Bhwgu fire, and Kuhn seems therefore to me quite right in comparing these Bhrigus with the Greek Phlegyes. Kuhn (p. 21) has collected the different forms of this name, such as Phlegys, Phlegyas, Phlegyeus, while Phlegyas is known as the name of a son of Ares and Chryse, a ruler of Phlegyantis near Orchomenos, who is said to have burnt the temple of Apollon, because his own daughter Koronis had 8lO BB.R1GAYANA. [chap.

through the god become the mother of Askl&pios. He was killed by Apollon with his arrows, and condemned to punishment in Hades. According to another legend, however, he was killed by Lykos and Nykteus (Lupus and Nocturnus), and is called the father or brother of Ixion. Few scholars would deny that Phlegys is the correct representative of Bhn'gu in Greek. Kuhn has also shown that there is a certain connection between the Phlegyes and Prometheus, because it was in the home of the Phlegyes, in Panopeus in Phokis, that Prometheus is said to have found the earth from which he formed the first man. This is, as we shall see, an important point, and may help us in interpreting the character of Prometheus, as a fire-god, in his various characters as discoverer of fire, benefactor of mankind by the gift of fire, nay maker of the human race. If others prefer to call Prometheus a fire-totem, no one would object, if only it would help us to a better understanding of Prometheus.
Prometheus.

In trying to disentangle the mythological web that surrounds Prometheus, we have to follow Kuhn, whose treatise on Die Herabkunft des Feuers is a masterwork that has been criticised by many, though understood by but few. It is well known that I do not always agree with Kuhn, but my admiration for his real genius has never wavered, and I fully expect that his work will in the future receive far greater recognition than it has in the past.

Kuhn has shown that etymologically Prometheus would represent the Sanskrit Pramanthu, the name of a person of whom little is known, but whose Vl] PROMETHEUS. 8ll name is closely connected with pramantha, which means the stick used for drilling wood (terebratio), and producing fire from the wood. The root manth or math means to stir, to twirl, and particularly to produce fire by vigorously rubbing one wood against another. Atharvan, Matarisvan, gods and men, perform this action. Math is also used in the more general sense of shaking, tearing, treating with violence, rolling. An old noun math, n. sing, manthas, means the stick used for turning milk into butter (mantha-dancZa), but it also means wind and thunderbolt, the thunderstorm being compared with any noisy action on earth, whether churning butter, rattling a cart, or playing at nine-pins. In Greek the root math appears in fxavOdva), to learn. Kuhn thinks that to learn meant to appropriate with violence, to carry off knowledge ; I think it would be more natural to assign to it the original meaning of rubbing, grinding, handling, studying (cf. mris, to stroke, to grasp, to consider, to study). But this is a point that cannot be settled, for the meaning of ' to grind ' or ' to rub it in ' in modern English would not help us much.

As to Prometheus for Promantheus1, his original meaning was that of pramantha or pramathyu, one who rubs and by rubbing produces fire 2, not one who robbed the fire from Zeus (p. 13). Popular etymology, however, soon discovered in Prometheus the clever producer of fire, the meaning of providens or prudens, nay assigned to Prometheus, Forethought, a brother Epimetheus, Afterthought. The 1 A Zeus Promantheus is mentioned by Kuhn (p. 17; from Lycophron, p. 537.
2 On p. 24 Kuhn seems to adopt the same view.
VOL. II. C C 8l2 PROMETHEUS. [chap.

transition from Prometheus, the producer of fire, to Prometheus, the discoverer of fire, the fire-god, requires no further explanation after what we saw before in the case of Angiras, Atharvan, Bhrigu, &c. Even SavitW, the sun, is said to have made the sun1.

Matarisvan, the wind, who is said to have brought fire from heaven to earth, is in other places himself the fire. Though Matarisvan has no longer the meaning of wind in the Veda, it should be remembered that Matali, the charioteer of Indra, is the son of the wind, and Matarisvan was probably a name of the Wind-dog, and not, as Roth supposes, of 'the lightning, swelling (svan) in his mother (matari), i. e. in the cloud,' — an impossible form.

If we have once recognised in Prometheus another form of Agni, a Titan who created the fire, who gave the fire to men, who brought this divine gift from the sky, we can better understand why he should share certain characteristics in common with Phoroneus, with Hephaistos. with Matarisvan, with BhWgu, with Atharvan and Angiras. Behind all these names there is the same thought, though modified according to circumstances. Prometheus is not Hephaistos nor Phoroneus. Hephaistos, the god, stands highest, Phoroneus, the king, stands lowest, and between the two there is room for the Titan Prometheus.

If then in Prometheus also we have to recognise a fire-god and a hero who produced the fire, we should expect that like Phoroneus or like Agni himself, he should also be the first of men, the creator of men, nay in the end the creator of all things, bhiivana ^andyan, as we read in Bv. VII, 5, 7.
1 Asval. £rauta IV, 6, 3. Oldenberg, Kel. des Veda, p. 449.

Vl] PROMETHEUS. 813 And so he is. Prometheus is not only fabled to have fashioned the first human beings, he is also represented as the father of Deukalion, who after the deluge became the father of the whole Hellenic race. We often see how mythological characters, starting from the same point, diverge afterwards in very different directions. What helps us in tracing them back to their common source is their performing the same acts, though in a different manner. No two characters can be more widely different than Prometheus and Hephaistos. But not only do both bring fire, but both perform the same office to Zeus by breaking open his head for the birth of Athene, and again both offer violence to Athene. Such coincidences cannot be ignored ; they are full of significance, and tend to confirm the hypothesis that both were representatives of Agni, more particularly of Agni as the Deus matutinus, a frequent character of the Vedic Agni, the Agni aushasa, or the daybreak. What Prometheus shares in common with the two is the bringing fire to men, conferring benefits on them, and being represented as their ancestor, if not their maker.
Minys, Manu.

Starting from this Kuhn takes another step which has been much criticised, but which seems to me extremely well established. Kuhn shows that in India Manu also is the ancestor of the human race, and he appeals to Otfried Miiller as having proved that Phlegyans and Minyans were only different names for the same race 1. If then the Phlegyans were descended from Phlegys, i.e. Bhri'gu, the 1 0. Miiller, Orchomenos, p. 179. C C 2 814 MINYS, MANU. [chap.

Minyans would be the descendants of *Mmys, i.e. Manu. The phonetic difficulties of this equation have been so carefully considered *, and, as far as I can judge, so completely removed by Kuhn, that I need not go over the same ground again. If scholars would only spend half the time on removing apparent phonetic difficulties which they spend on starting fresh ones, the progress of Comparative Mythology would have been much more rapid, and at the same time much more satisfactory. That Sanskrit a should be represented by Greek i is certainly not regular, but we have only to think of asva = 177-7709 to see that it is possible even in appellative nouns. The long i of Minos does not vitiate the equations MavFavr = M^gjs and MavFa = Mtvva, and the fact that Minos, like Prometheus, is sometimes represented as the father of Deukalion ought not to be overlooked. There is much confusion, however, in the stories about Minos. Originally there was only one, but afterwards there were two, and it is not easy to keep them always apart. That both Minos and Manu were famous as lawgivers need not mean more than that the ancient laws were naturally referred to the first ancestors or the ancient kings of certain countries. But though in some respects there is similarity between Manu and Minos, and though in Sanskrit Manu and BbWgu are closely connected, there is no trace of an igneous character in Manu, nor, as far as we can judge, in Minos.
Manu. When we come to know Manu in the ancient poetry of India, he is already man, the father man 1 Beitrage, vol. i, p. 369.

Vl] MANU 815 (manush-pita), or, as we should say, the type of mankind. He has generally been interpreted etymologically as the thinker, from man, to think ; but this has been declared to be far too abstract a name for the early periods of Aryan thought. Of course, the ancient mythological names may have passed through thousands of years and thousands of changes before they meet our view. If we want, however, for Manu a physical background, we can hardly find it in the fire, but rather, I think, in the moon. The moon was called the measurer of time, and it is possible, though I say no more, that the name Manu was coined originally, like the Greek jjLrjv and fx-qurj, as a name of the moon. There is a curious but obscure passage in the Eig-veda, IV, 26, 1, beginning with the words, Aham ManuA abhavam Surya/i Aa, ' I was Manu and I was the sun.' Here the meaning of moon suggests itself, but as the poet goes on to say he was also the Rishi Kakshivat and Kavi Usanas, we cannot gain much information about the ancient meaning of Manu from this single passage. Nor do we gain much light from another verse, X, 62, 11, where we read of Manu's dakshma as suryena yatamana. We should, however, remember the story of Saranyu and Vivasvat, which tells us that when Saranyu fled from Vivasvat, a substitute for her was given to him, who became the mother of Manu. Now Mannhardt (1. c, p. 298) already has remarked that when in popular stories we hear of a substituted wife, we may generally take her to be meant for the night or the winter. If then this substituted wife is the night, her child would naturally be the moon. It is a popular idea, unsupported though it be by observation, that as soon as the sun sets the moon 816 MANU. [chap.
rises, and rises just opposite the setting sun. Thus Heine writes : —
Die gliihend rothe Sonne steigt Hinab ins weit aufschauernde Silbergraue Weltmeer.

Luftgebilde, rosig angehaucht, Wallen ihr nach ; und gegeniiber, Aus herbstlich dammerndem Wolkenschleiei, Ein traurig todtblasses Antlitz, Bricht hervor der Mond. But whatever the distant antecedents of Manu may have been, in the Rig-veda he is the father, also the leader of men (grkmamXh, X, 62, 11). He was an individual Manu, and in the #atapatha-brahma?ia this Manu is represented as the man who escaped from the flood. He is called Vivasvat, but also the son of Vivasvat, i.e. Vaivasvata, and a legend represents him as the son of Aditya and his second wife Savarna. Vivasvat is clearly a name of the sun, while the children, Yama and Yami, have been taken by some scholars as the progenitors of the human race. All this shows the solar, and if solar, then igneous, character of the mythological Manu, and it also explains to a certain extent why Matarisvan is said to have brought Agni from the sky to the BhWgus. But manu is also the regular appellative for man, though sometimes it is difficult to decide who is meant. When we read, Jtv. I, 36, 19: Ni tvam Agne manu/i dadhe gyotih #anaya sasvate, we may translate either ' Manu placed thee, O Agni,' or ' man placed thee, 0 Agni, as a light for all people.' Again, Rv. X, 63, 7 : YebhyaA hotrani prathamam aye^e manuA samiddhagniA, ' To whom Manu or man with lighted fire offered the first invocation.' This is a very incomplete account of Agni, but the vi] MANU. 817 outlines, as here given, are, T believe, correct, and can easily be filled out by further research. The thirty-second and forty-sixth volumes of the Sacred Books of the East contain a translation of more than half of the hymns addressed to Agni, and I regret that as the number of fifty volumes is not to be exceeded, the remaining hymns will not appear, at least not in that series.
Abstract Deities.

There is, however, one more lesson which the study of Agni should teach us, and which we should never forget in analysing other gods of the Veda. However high Agni may have risen as a god, as a benefactor and protector, nay as creator of heaven and earth, and source of light and life, he started originally from nature, from the visible fire on the hearth, or from the sun, or from the fiery meteor that descended from the clouds in the shape of lightning. What we know as a fact in this case we may safely extend to other cases. All Vedic gods, nay all Aryan gods, were in the beginning physical. I say in the beginning, for there came, no doubt, a time when the concept of deity being once formed and having become familiar, invisible and purely abstract objects also were raised to a divine status. Va&, speech, for instance, though not visible, was audible, but Mn'tyu, death, was merely a postulated being, and yet he also is invoked as a deity. And these cases can teach us a lesson. Mrityu, death, was evidently a secondary deity and grafted on a primary deity, viz. Yama, the setting sun, and evenVa&, speech, shows in the distant background a physical foundation in the voice of thunder.
8l8 ABSTRACT DEITIES. [chap.

It has been supposed that all female deities are purely abstract creations. This is not so. Some of them have their physical prototypes, such as Apas, the waters, whether on earth or in the sky, Ushas, the dawn, with the Apsarasas, PWthivi, the earth, Sarasvati, if representing the river, and with her I7a, Bharati, and possibly Mahi ; Kaka, Sinivali, Kuhu and Gungu, phases of the moon ; possibly Aditi, the Beyond, and PWsni, the cloudy sky, the mother of the Maruts. Others, such as Yami, Indrani, Agnayi, Varuwani, Asvini, Surya, Arawyani, are clearly derivative, not, however, Sarasvati. Purely abstract goddesses, however, are VaZ;, speech, /Sraddha, faith, Aramati, devotion, and these must be assigned to a secondary phase of the Vedic religion.

I quite admit that Varuna also has assumed a decidedly ethical and abstract character in the Vedic Pantheon, but I hold that in the beginning he must have been as concrete a deity as Agni or at least as Indra. Even if it should be questioned that like Ouranos he represented at first the sky, particularly the dark sky, there are sufficient traces left that point to him as the chief actor in the firmament of heaven. There can be no doubt that in later Sanskrit Varuna is a decidedly physical god, how then could we suppose that he should not have been so from the beginning, or should actually have been taken over ready made from a more spiritual Semitic religion ? In Post -Vedic literature Varuwa is clearly the god of the sea and of the Western quarter of the earth ; is it likely then that in the Veda he was a purely spiritual deity without any physical antecedents ? These antecedents may not be many nor very palpable, but we should remember Vl] ABSTRACT DEITIES. 819 that both in the Veda and in the Avesta the waters, Apas, are mentioned as the wives of Varu^a and of Ahura, and that the place especially assigned to him is called apya yoni, the watery place (Rv. II, $8, 9). Varima moves along with the waters (adbhiA yati VarunaA samudraiA, Hv. I, 161, 14); he is fire or the sun in the evening (saA varunay^ sayam agniA bhavati, Ath. IX, 3, 18) ; he actually created the night (mitral ahaA at/anayat, varmio ratrim, T. S. VI, 4, 8, 3), and hence abides in the West. In the prayers addressed he is not always asked for spiritual blessings only, but for the very gifts which a god of the sky is able to grant. Thus in a prayer for rain we read, Rv. V, 63 : —

' Guardians of the Law, ye whose nature is truth, you mount your chariot in the highest heaven ; 0 Mitra and Varu>m, whomsoever you protect here, to him the rain brings down the sweet water from heaven.'
zh: 這個由摩多里斯凡（Matarisvan）——毗瓦斯瓦特（Vivasvat）的使者——通過摩擦而產生的光輝之物，顯然是阿格尼（Agni）。但在這種情況下，如同在阿塔爾瓦（Atharvan）的情況一樣，我們不再發現毗瑞古（Bhrigu）作為某位神祇的名號，而只是作為其崇拜者的名號，即古代毗瑞古人（Bhrigus）的種族。據說毗瑞古人發現了阿格尼（X, 46, 2），將他帶給人類（I, 58, 6），將他藏在樹林中（VI, 15, 2）。他們從摩多里斯凡（風神）那裡接受了他，後者在阿格尼被隱沒時點燃了他（III, 5, 10）。在後來的時代，毗瑞古人主要作為印度的一個著名家族而為人所知，就像安吉拉斯（Aiigiras）家族和阿塔爾瓦人（Atharvans）一樣（Bv. X, 14, 6），許多聖者（Rishis），如紹那卡（Saunaka），以及英雄，如帕拉蘇拉瑪（Parasurama），都為自己屬於該家族而感到自豪。當克里希那（Krishna）想要說自己是或曾是一切事物中最優越的時候，他說在聖者中他是毗瑞古（《薄伽梵歌》X, 25）。在所有這些中可能隱含著圖騰崇拜的跡象，儘管我必須承認我看不出來。該名號的語源學解釋為古代吠陀詮釋家所知，耶斯卡（Yaska）（《語源論》III, 17）說：「毗瑞古生於光中，毗瑞古雖然被烤焦或灼傷，卻未在煤炭中燒焦。安吉拉人是安吉亞人（煤炭），他們之所以如此被稱呼是因為它們做記號。」

與庫恩（第18頁）及其他學者不同，我將 dehe 理解為非 deha 的位置格，而是 dah 的完成式被動分詞，意為「被燃燒」。根 hhrig 或 bhṛgg（bhriggsiti）在梵文中意為「烘烤」或「油炸」，雅斯卡（Yaska）由此衍生出婆羅笈多（Bhṛgu），此根若非恰好相同，至少與其他表示「發光」或「熾燃」的根密切相關，如梵文 bhr%「發光」，亦如 bhargas（光采），以及希臘文（φλέγω）（φλόξ）、拉丁文 fulgere（fulgur）、flagrare（flamma）。加熱與發光乃同源概念，如梵文 suryas tapati「太陽閃耀」，字面意為「太陽溫暖」，而油炸的舉動被理解為加熱與烤炙的行為，尚非烹飪或煮沸。

如果阿格尼（Agni）可被稱為 bhrgavawa「光輝的」或 bhṛgu，我們現在就能輕易理解這個名字如何成為某一種族祖先的名字，被稱為婆羅笈多族（Bhṛgus），因為他們擁有婆羅笈多之火，庫恩因此在比較這些婆羅笈多人與希臘的弗勒癸埃人（Phlegyes）時似乎完全正確。庫恩（第21頁）蒐集了這個名字的各種形式，如弗勒癸斯（Phlegys）、弗勒癸亞斯（Phlegyas）、弗勒癸烏斯（Phlegyeus），而弗勒癸亞斯已為人所知，乃是阿瑞斯與克律塞之子、統治奧爾科墨諾斯附近弗勒癸亞蒂斯地區的一位統治者的名字，據傳他因自己的女兒科羅尼斯而焚毀了阿波羅的廟宇。

通過該神而成為阿斯克勒庇俄斯（Askl&pios）的母親。他被阿波羅用箭射殺，並被判在哈得斯受罰。然而根據另一個傳說，他被呂科斯和尼克透斯（Lupus 和 Nocturnus）所殺，並被稱為伊克西翁的父親或兄長。少有學者會否認斐勒革斯是梵文 Bhn'gu 在希臘文中的正確代表。庫恩也已經證明，斐勒革人與普羅米修斯之間存在著某種聯繫，因為據說普羅米修斯正是在斐勒革人的家鄉——福基斯的帕諾佩烏斯——發現了用來塑造第一個人類的土壤。正如我們將要看到的，這是一個重要的點，並可能幫助我們解釋普羅米修斯作為火神的性質，其體現在發現火、以火的恩賜造福人類、甚至創造人類種族的各個方面。如果其他人寧願稱普羅米修斯為火圖騰（fire-totem），只要這有助於我們更好地理解普羅米修斯，沒有人會反對。
普羅米修斯（Prometheus）
在試圖理清圍繞普羅米修斯的神話網絡時，我們必須追隨庫恩（Kuhn）的論著《火的降臨》（Die Herabkunft des Feuers），這部傑作雖經許多人批評，卻少有人真正理解。眾所周知，我並非總是贊同庫恩的觀點，但對他真才實學的欽佩從未動搖，我完全相信他的著作將來必會獲得遠超過去的認可。

庫恩已經證明，普羅米修斯（Prometheus）在語源學上代表梵文 Pramanthu，這是一位鮮為人知的人物之名，但其名號與 pramantha 密切相連，後者指的是用來鑽木取火的木棍（terebratio）。根 manth 或 math 的意思是攪動、旋轉，尤其是指通過猛烈摩擦一根木頭與另一根木頭來取火。雅塔婆（Atharvan）、瑪塔里希梵（Matarisvan）等諸神和人類都從事此類活動。Math 一詞也用於更廣泛的意義，如搖晃、撕扯、暴力對待、翻滾。古老的名詞 math（複數 manthas）指的是用來將牛奶攪拌成黃油的轉棍（mantha-danda），但它也表示風和雷電，雷暴被比作地上任何嘈雜的動作，無論是攪黃油、車轆隆隆作響，還是玩九柱戲。在希臘文中，根 math 出現在 μανθάνω 一詞中，意為學習。庫恩認為學習的本義是暴力地佔有、奪取知識；我則認為將其歸於揉搓、研磨、掌握、研究的原始含義會更為自然（參 mris，撫摸、抓取、思考、研究）。但這是一個無法確定的問題，因為現代英文中「研磨」或「用力摩擦」的含義對我們的幫助不大。

關於普羅米修斯（Promantheus）¹，其原始意義乃是 pramantha 或 pramathyu，即摩擦以及通過摩擦生火者²，而非從宙斯手中盜火者（第 13 頁）。然而，民間語源學很快就在普羅米修斯中發現了巧妙的生火者，其含義為 providens 或 prudens，甚且將先見之明（Forethought）賦予普羅米修斯，並為其安排了一位兄弟厄庇米修斯（Epimetheus），意為後思（Afterthought）。¹ 庫恩曾提及宙斯·普羅曼透斯（Zeus Promantheus）（第 17 頁；出自呂科芬，第 537 頁）。
第24頁庫恩似乎採納了相同的觀點。
第二卷 C C 8l2 普羅米修斯 [章]
從普羅米修斯作為火的製造者轉變為普羅米修斯作為火的發現者、火神，在我們之前透過安吉拉斯（Angiras）、阿闥婆（Atharvan）、婆利古（Bhrigu）等案例所見之後，已無需進一步說明。甚至連日神薩維德（Savitṛ）本身，據說也創造了太陽。
馬塔里斯凡（Matarisvan），即風神，據說曾將火焰從天界帶往人間，但在其他地方卻本身即為火。雖然馬塔里斯凡在《吠陀》中已不具風的含義，然而應當記得，因陀羅的御者馬塔利乃風神之子，馬塔里斯凡可能是風犬的名號，而非如羅特所推測的「閃電，在其母（matari）即雲中膨脹（svan）」——此乃不可能的形式。

如果我們一旦在普羅米修斯身上認出了阿格尼的另一種形式，一位創造火焰的泰坦，一位將火焰賦予人類、將這份神聖的禮物從天界帶下來的泰坦，我們就能更好地理解，他為何應當與弗洛雷烏斯、赫淮斯托斯、馬塔瑞斯瓦恩、婆笈（Bhrigu）、阿闥婆（Atharvan）和昂吉羅斯（Angiras）等諸神分享某些共同的特徵。在所有這些名字背後，存在著同一個思想，雖然根據不同的情況而有所變化。普羅米修斯並非赫淮斯托斯，亦非弗洛雷烏斯。作為神祇的赫淮斯托斯位居最高，作為國王的弗洛雷烏斯位居最低，而泰坦普羅米修斯就夾在這二者之間。
如果我們必須在普羅米修斯身上認出一位火神，以及一位生產火焰的英雄，那麼我們應該期待，就像弗洛雷烏斯或阿格尼本身一樣，他也應該是人類之首、人類的創造者，乃至最終是萬物的創造者，bhiivana ^andyan，如我們在《梨俱吠陀》第七卷第五經第七頌（Bv. VII, 5, 7）所讀到的。
梨俱吠陀祭祀經 IV, 6, 3。歐登貝格《吠陀宗教》，第 449 頁。

普羅米修斯確實如此。普羅米修斯不僅在神話中被傳說為塑造第一批人類的工匠，他也被描繪為代卡利翁（Deukalion）的父親，而代卡利翁在洪水之後成為整個海倫尼克種族的祖父。我們常能看到神話人物如何從同一源點出發，隨後卻各自沿著截然不同的方向發展。幫助我們追溯它們回到共同源頭的，是它們雖然以不同的方式執行相同的行為。沒有兩個人物能比普羅米修斯和赫菲斯托斯（Hephaistos）更加南轅北轍。但不只兩者都帶來火焰，更重要的是，兩者都對宙斯（Zeus）行同樣的職能——為雅典娜（Athene）的誕生而劈開他的頭顱，同時兩者也都對雅典娜施暴。這類巧合不可被忽視；它們充滿了意義，傾向於確認下述假說：兩者都代表阿格尼（Agni），尤其是代表作為曙光之神（Deus matutinus）的阿格尼，這是吠陀阿格尼的常見性質，即阿格尼‧奧沙莎（Agni aushasa），或黎明之火。普羅米修斯與這兩者共同之處在於將火帶給人類、造福於他們，以及被描繪為他們的祖先（若非造物者的話）。
密紐斯，瑪努。

基於此，庫恩邁出了另一步，雖然遭致多方批評，但在我看來卻是極為確鑿的。庫恩證明了在印度，瑪奴（Manu）同樣是人類的祖先，他引用奧弗里德‧穆勒（Otfried Müller）已證明的說法，即弗勒吉安人（Phlegyans）與民尼安人（Minyans）只是同一個種族的不同名稱¹。若弗勒吉安人確實是源自弗勒吉斯（Phlegys），亦即婆利吉（Bhri'gu），那麼 ¹ 奧‧穆勒（O. Müller）《俄科美諾斯》（Orchomenos），第179頁。

民尼亞人（Minyans）應為*密謬斯（Mmys），即馬奴（Manu）的後代。這一等式的音韻難題已被庫恩（Kuhn）經過精心推敲而完全消除，據我所知，無需我再次費力討論。若學者們肯花費一半的時間來消除表面上的音韻難題，而不是不斷製造新的難題，比較神話學的進展必將快速許多，同時也更加令人滿意。梵文 a 用希臘文 i 來表示固然不是常規，但我們只需想到 asva = 177-7709 就能看出，即使在普通名詞中也是可能的。米諾斯（Minos）的長 i 並不足以推翻 MavFavr = M^gjs 和 MavFa = Mtvva 這兩個等式，而且米諾斯如同普羅米修斯（Prometheus）一樣，有時被描繪為德卡利翁（Deukalion）的父親，這一事實不應被忽視。然而，關於米諾斯的故事中存在許多混亂。最初只有一個米諾斯，後來卻有了兩個，並不容易始終將他們區分開來。馬奴和米諾斯都以立法者聞名，這不過意味著古代的法律自然被歸於某些國家的最初祖先或古代國王罷了。儘管在某些方面馬奴和米諾斯之間確有相似之處，儘管在梵文中馬奴和布拉赫曼（BbWgu）關係密切，但馬奴身上沒有任何火焰般的特性，據我們所知，米諾斯身上也沒有。
瑪奴。當我們在古代印度詩歌中認識瑪奴時，他已經是人類——人類之父了。¹ ¹ *Beiträge*, vol. i, p. 369.

VI] 曼奴（manush-pita），或如我們所言，乃人類之型範。向來學者多從詞源學角度詮釋，謂曼奴源於「man」（思考），故為「思考者」之義；但此說已被指摘過於抽象，不宜為雅利安思想早期之名稱。當然，上古神話名稱可能經歷數千年與數千般變化，方才呈現於我們眼前。然若我們欲為曼奴尋求物質背景，則難以在火中得之，反而我認為應在月亮中求。月亮被稱為時間之測量者，故有可能（雖然我不敢妄言）曼奴之名原係如希臘文 μήν 與 μήνη 一樣，作為月亮之名稱而創造出來的。梨俱吠陀（Rig-veda）第四卷第26首第1句有一段奇異而晦澀之文辭，開篇云「Aham Manu abhavam Surya iti」，意為「我曾是曼奴，我曾是太陽」。此處月亮之義自然浮現，然詩人隨後又言其亦曾是仙人迦克希瓦特（Rishi Kakshivat）與詩聖烏沙納斯（Kavi Usanas），故吾人難以由此單一段落獲得關於曼奴上古義涵之充分信息。另一詩句（第十卷第62首第11句）亦所獲不多，其文云曼奴之祭祀（dakshma）為「suryena yatamana」。然吾人應當記取莎蘭努與毘毘斯瓦特（Saranyu and Vivasvat）之故事，其云當莎蘭努自毘毘斯瓦特而逃之時，一替身妻被賜予他，此替身妻遂成為曼奴之母。曼哈特（Mannhardt）（前引書，第298頁）已曾指出，在民間故事中，凡聞有妻子被替身者，其通常可視為夜或冬之代表。若此替身妻為夜晚，則其子自應為月亮。此乃一常俗之想法，雖未獲觀測所支持，然謂太陽一沒，即生月亮，此說頗為普遍。
上升，並且恰好在落日的相反方向上升。因此海涅寫道：—
我注意到提供的「英文原文」實際上是德文，而非英文。根據您的指示第2點，我只應翻譯英文原文，附加的德文僅供消歧義參考，不應翻譯德文。 請提供實際的英文原文，我將依照您的翻譯規則進行繁體中文翻譯。

雲氣繚繞，玫瑰色氤氳；它們飄然而去；而相對地，自秋日昏暗的雲幕中，一張悲哀蒼白如死的面容破曉而出——月亮。然而，無論摩奴（Manu）的遠古淵源如何，在《梨俱吠陀》中，他是人的父親，也是人的領導者（grkmamXh, X, 62, 11）。他是一位個別的摩奴，在《百道梵書》（Satapatha-brahmana）中，此摩奴被描繪為逃脫洪水的人。他被稱為毘瓦斯瓦特（Vivasvat），但也是毘瓦斯瓦特之子，即毘瓦斯瓦塔（Vaivasvata），一則傳說將他表現為阿迪蒂婭（Aditya）及其第二任妻子薩瓦爾那（Savarna）的兒子。毘瓦斯瓦特明顯是太陽的名稱，而其子女夜摩（Yama）和夜彌（Yami）已被某些學者視為人類的祖先。這一切都表明神話中的摩奴具有太陽的性質，而若具有太陽的性質，則具有火的性質，這在某種程度上也解釋了為何馬塔里什瓦（Matarisvan）據說從天空將阿格尼（Agni）帶給婆利居人（Bhṛgus）。但摩奴（manu）也是人的常規通用名詞，雖然有時難以決定所指者為誰。當我們讀到《梨俱吠陀》I, 36, 19：「Ni tvam Agne manuṣi dadhe jyotiḥ śanaya sasvate」時，我們可以譯為「摩奴為你安置，啊阿格尼」，或「人為你安置，啊阿格尼，作為所有人民的光芒」。再者，《梨俱吠陀》X, 63, 7：「Yebhyaḥ hotrāṇi prathamaṃ ayeṣe manuḥ samiddha-agniḥ」，「摩奴或人以燃燒的火向其獻上最初的祈禱」。這只是對阿格尼一個極不完整的記述，但我相信此處所給的輪廓是正確的，可容易地通過進一步的研究加以填補。《東方聖典集成》（Sacred Books of the East）第三十二卷和第四十六卷包含了獻給阿格尼的超過一半讚歌的譯文，而我遺憾地表示，由於五十卷的數量限制，其餘讚歌將不會出現，至少不會在該系列中出現。
抽象神靈

然而，對於阿格尼（Agni）的研究還有一課應當教導我們，在分析吠陀的其他提婆時，我們永遠不應遺忘這一課。無論阿格尼作為提婆上升到何等高度，作為恩人與保護者，甚至作為天地的創造者，以及光明與生命的源頭，他最初源自於自然——源自爐火上看得見的火，或源自太陽，或源自以閃電形態從雲層降落的熾熱流星。我們在此案例中所知悉的事實，我們可以安全地推及其他案例。所有吠陀提婆，乃至所有雅利安提婆，在最初都是物理的、自然的。我說「在最初」，因為無疑曾有過一個時期，當提神的概念業已形成並變得熟悉時，無形的與純粹抽象的對象也被提升至神聖的地位。例如，毘（Vāc，言辭），雖然不可見，但卻是可聞的；然而末梨笈（Mṛtyu，死亡）僅是一個假設的存在，然而他也被祈禱為一位提婆。這些案例可以教導我們一堂課。末梨笈（Mrityu，死亡）顯然是一位次級的提婆，移植於一位原始提婆之上，即夜摩（Yama），沒落的太陽；甚至毘（Vāc，言辭）也在遠處的背景中展現出物理的基礎，源於雷聲的嗓音。
818 抽象提婆。[章

人們一直以為所有女性提婆都純粹是抽象的創造物。事實並非如此。其中有些具有其物質原型，如阿帕斯（Apas）──水，無論是地上的水或天空中的水；烏莎斯（Ushas）──黎明，以及阿普薩羅斯（Apsarasas）；普里媞維（Pṛthivi）──大地；莎拉斯瓦蒂（Sarasvati），若代表河流，則與她相關的有伊拉（Ila）、巴拉蒂（Bharati）及可能的瑪希（Mahi）；卡卡（Kaka）、西尼瓦麗（Sinivali）、庫胡（Kuhu）與貢古（Gungu），這些是月亮的諸種相位；可能還有阿迪蒂（Aditi）──彼岸者，以及布什尼（Pūṣṇi）──多雲的天空，馬魯茲（Maruts）的母親。其他的，如亞彌（Yami）、因陀羅妮（Indrani）、阿格納伊（Agnayi）、伐樓那妮（Varuṇani）、阿濕溫妮（Aśvini）、蘇利耶（Surya）、阿拉瓦尼（Arayāṇi），明顯是衍生的，但莎拉斯瓦蒂不在此列。純粹的抽象女神則有瓦克（Vāc）──言語、什拉達（Śraddha）──信仰、阿拉瑪蒂（Aramati）──虔誠，這些必須歸屬於吠陀宗教的次級階段。

我完全承認在吠陀諸神體系中，婆樓那（Varuna）確實已經具備了明確的倫理性和抽象特徵，但我主張他在最初必定是一位如同阿耆尼（Agni）一樣具體的提婆，至少也與因陀羅（Indra）一樣具體。即便有人質疑他如同烏剌諾斯（Ouranos）一樣最初代表天空、特別是陰暗的天空，仍然有足夠的痕跡表明他是蒼穹中的首要行動者。毫無疑問，在後期梵文中，婆樓那是一位確定不移的物質神；那麼我們怎能設想他從最初就不是如此呢？或者說他應該被直接從更具精神性的閃語族宗教中挪用過來？在吠陀後期文獻中，婆樓那顯然是海洋之神，也是大地西方四方的神祇；那麼在吠陀中他怎可能是一位沒有任何物質前身的純粹精神提婆呢？這些物質前身也許數量不多，也許也不太明顯，但我們應當記住，無論在吠陀還是在阿維斯塔（Avesta）中，水（Apas）都被提到是婆樓那和阿胡拉（Ahura）的妻子，而特別指定給他的地方被稱為「apya yoni」，即水的源處（梨俱吠陀 II, 28, 9）。婆樓那隨著水流而行動（adbhiḥ yati Varunaḥ samudrai, 梨俱吠陀 I, 161, 14）；他是黃昏時的火或太陽（sa varunaya sayam agni bhavati，雅柔吠陀 IX, 3, 18）；他實際上創造了夜晚（mitra aham adhanat, varuno ratrim，泰迪利亞梵書 VI, 4, 8, 3），因此安居於西方。在對他的禱告中，人們並非總是只求精神上的祝福，而是祈求一位天空之神能夠賜予的各種恩賜。因此在求雨的禱告中，我們讀到梨俱吠陀 V, 63：——
「守法者啊，汝等其性本真，汝等乘坐汝等之戰車於最高天界；喔，密多羅與跋樓拿啊，汝等在此所護佑之人，雨水為其自天上帶來甘甜之水。」
